text: The Imperially Approved Complete Library, Volume 12, Subcategory of the Novelist Category: Miscellaneous Stories - Preface We officials respectfully present the *Miscellaneous Records of Emperor Ming* in two volumes and the *Supplementary Records* in one volume, compiled by Zheng Chuhui of the Tang dynasty. Zheng Chuhui, courtesy name Yanmei, was from Yingyang and the grandson of the chancellor Zheng Yuqing. He passed the imperial examination in the eighth year of the Taihe era and rose to the position of Censor-in-Inspector of the Ministry of Justice and Military Governor of the Xuanwu Army. His biography is attached to the *Old Book of Tang* entry on Zheng Yuqing. This work was completed in the ninth year of the Dazhong era and records various miscellaneous events. According to historical records, when Zheng Chuhui was serving as a proofreader, he compiled three volumes of *Miscellaneous Records of Emperor Ming*, which circulated in the world. Zhao Gongwu's *Records of Books* mentions *Miscellaneous Records of Emperor Ming* in two volumes, but also states that there is a *Supplementary Record* in one volume, which includes twelve events recorded by the Censor-in-Inspector. Zhao Gongwu counts both the main text and the supplementary volume together, while Ye Mengde in his *Summer Retreat Notes* says that Zheng Chuhui's *Miscellaneous Records of Emperor Ming* records the dispute between Zhang Qujiang and Li Linfu over the real estate of Niu Xianke. At that time, it was autumn, and the emperor ordered Gao Lishi to give a white feather fan to Zhang Qujiang, who, feeling anxious, wrote a poem to present. It seemed to say that Emperor Ming was about to be dismissed for disobeying orders, hence the gift of the fan in autumn to convey his intention. The *New Book of Tang* included this event in the biography.

According to the preface of the *Qujiang Collection*, in the 24th year of Kaiyuan during the midsummer, the Grand General Gao Lishi was granted white feather fans to the Chancellors, and Zhang Jiuling was among them. This was not a fall gift, and generally speaking, Li Linfu was also involved, not exclusively for Qujiang. Thus, it is known that stories in novels, if not based on firsthand experience, cannot be lightly recorded. As mentioned above, the book by Chu Hui is also not entirely factual, but the stories in novels mix true and false, which has been the case since ancient times, not just in Chu Hui's work. One must conduct thorough research and carefully select the content, and should not discard the entire book because of a few inaccuracies. The *Records of Summer Retreat* also states that Lu Huishen practiced frugality, and his home had no ornaments of precious gems, jade, silk, or embroidery. This was indeed a good practice, but historical records state that his wife and children were cold and hungry, and when Cui Yan and others passed by his door, they did not use screens, and when it rained or snowed, they would pull out mats to shield themselves. It is likely that this account is derived from Zheng Chuwei's *Miscellaneous Records of Emperor Ming*, and the historians erroneously believed it. The current version of the text does not include this passage, and thus some parts have been lost, making it not a complete volume. December of the 46th year of Qianlong's reign, respectfully proofread and submitted.

The Chief Compilers: Minister Ji Yun, Minister Lu Xixiong, Minister Sun Shiyi The Chief Proofreader: Minister Lu Feich Imperially Ordered Four Treasuries of Literature Miscellaneous Records of the Ming Emperor, Volume One Compiled by Zheng Chuhui of the Tang Dynasty During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of the Tang Dynasty, when Fang Guan was the magistrate of Lü County, Xing Zhen Hépú came from Mount Tai. Fang Guan treated him with utmost respect and courtesy. They walked together leisurely, and before they knew it, they had traveled dozens of miles to the village of Xiagu. There, they encountered an abandoned Buddhist temple with dense pine and bamboo trees. Hépú sat beneath a pine tree and struck the ground with his staff, instructing his attendant to dig several feet deep. They uncovered a jar containing letters written by Lou Shide and Yong Gong. Hépú smiled and asked Fang, "Do you recognize these?" Fang suddenly remembered his past life as a monk, and Hépú explained that Yong Gong was his former self. Hépú then told Fang, "When you die, it will be because you ate fish. After your death, you will be buried in a coffin made of paulownia wood. You will not die in your private residence, nor in a public hall, nor in a Taoist temple, nor in the home of relatives or friends. Later, you will be exiled to Lang Prefecture and will stay in the Zijigong Temple. After several days of illness, the magistrate of the prefecture suddenly prepared fish and invited Fang to his official residence. Fang happily agreed and went there. After finishing the meal, he returned home and died suddenly. The prefect ordered Fang's body to be placed in the temple, and the coffin was made of paulownia wood.

During the late Kaiyuan era, there was a man named Sun from Hangzhou who was skilled in physiognomy. He traveled to Muzhou, where the prefect ordered a thorough physiognomic assessment of all local officials. At that time, Fang Guan served as the head of the household registry, and Cui Huan, who had been demoted from a county magistrate in the area around Chang'an to the deputy governor of Tonglu County, was also among the officials. Sun said, "Both of these gentlemen will reach high-ranking positions. However, Fang will control the symbols of imperial power, and Cui will later serve as the governor of Hangzhou. Although I may not see him, he will surely bestow his favor on me." Indeed, after Fang was appointed as a high-ranking official and carried the imperial edict from Sichuan to Lingwu to present it to Emperor Suzong, Cui Huan eventually became the governor of Hangzhou. Upon his arrival, he inquired about Sun and found that he had passed away just ten days earlier. Cui then appointed Sun's son as a military officer and provided grain and silk to support his family. During the Kaiyuan era, Emperor Xuanzong was eager to improve governance and paid particular attention to selecting capable ministers. He often wanted to appoint Zhang Jiazheng as chancellor but had forgotten his name. One night, he ordered a eunuch to carry a candle into the government offices to find out who was on duty that night. The eunuch returned and reported to the Minister of Personnel, Wei Kang. The emperor then summoned Wei Kang to the palace. The emperor said, "I wish to appoint a chancellor. I remember his demeanor as a prominent minister of the time, now serving as a military governor in the north. I do not want to consult others about it. I have thought about it for ten days but still cannot recall his name. Please tell me." Wei Kang replied, "Zhang Qizhou is currently the military governor of Shuofang." The emperor immediately ordered him to draft an edict and instructed palace attendants to hold candles while Wei Kang knelt before him and wrote the decree.

The emperor highly praised Zhang Kang's agility and elegance, and then hurriedly ordered him to write the edict and return to the palace. The emperor did not undress and waited, as he was about to issue the edict. It was still early in the night when suddenly a court eunuch came to summon Zhang Kang again. The emperor greeted him and said, "It was not Zhang Qi, but Zhang Jiachen, the military governor of Taiyuan, who was specifically tasked with drafting the edict." The emperor said to Zhang Kang, "Only when my mind is set can I speak and issue orders. Just now, while reviewing recent memorials from the ministers, I came across one from Zhang Jiachen, and only then did I remember his name. This is also divinely inspired, not due to human effort." The emperor praised his ability to find the right person and also sighed that appointments and dismissals should be made by someone with authority. Su Ting was exceptionally intelligent and perceptive, able to read thousands of words in a day. Although he had a remarkable memory, his father, Su Gui, was very strict and often made him wear a blue cotton shirt and lie on the bed, exposing his neck to receive punishment. By the time he grew up, he had become a leading scholar of his time. He was naturally carefree and enjoyed drinking. When Emperor Xuanzong had pacified the internal turmoil and wanted to draft an edict, he found it difficult to find the right person and asked Su Gui, "Who can draft the edict? Think about it." Su Gui replied, "I don't know about others, but my son Su Ting is very agile and can be relied upon. However, he enjoys drinking, so please ensure he doesn't get drunk, and he can handle the task." Emperor Xuanzong immediately summoned him. When Su Ting arrived, he was still a bit drunk from the previous night and performed a clumsy bow, even vomiting in the palace. The emperor ordered him to lie down in front of him and personally covered him with a quilt. After he sobered up, he took the paper and wrote it down immediately, producing a splendid and eloquent text.

Emperor Xuanzong was greatly pleased by the orderly and comprehensive nature of the work, and he patted the man's back, saying, "No one knows a son better than his father. Could he be like this?" From then on, he esteemed him highly and intended to appoint him to a significant position. Wei Sili was appointed as Grand Secretary, and Gua was entrusted with the task of drafting the official announcement, which was written by Ting. Xue Ji contributed his calligraphy, and at that time, people referred to their combined talents as the "Three Excesses." Ting was skilled in speech, and when the Prefect of Jingzhao passed by, Gua ordered Ting to compose a poem about the prefect. Ting replied, "Though ugly, it has feet, but its claws do not reach its body. Seeing that you have no mouth, I know you are a rare person." Gua and the Daoist priest Zhou Yanyun from Dongming Temple had been in frequent contact. Zhou once wished to erect a stele in honor of his master and said to Gua, "To fulfill my wish, I would only trouble you and your sons—Fifth Son, Wen, Sixth Son, Shu, and Seventh Son, Sheng, to inscribe the stone." Gua laughed but did not speak, yet he was deeply impressed by Zhou's public spirit. Gua's sons included Ting, the fifth son, Shen, the sixth son, and Bing, the seventh son, who was skilled in the Eight-Stroke Script. Emperor Xuanzong held a grand ceremony at the Qinzhen Pavilion, with music and performances by a hundred entertainers. At that time, there was a performer named Wang Dainiang in the Imperial Music Office who was skilled in balancing on a hundred-foot pole, on which she placed a wooden mountain resembling the mythical islands of Yingzhou and Fangzhang. She made children hold red banners and enter and exit between the poles, dancing continuously without pause. At that time, Liu Yan, a child prodigy, was appointed as the Imperial Scribe at the age of ten. Though his appearance was unconventional and somewhat ugly, his intelligence and wisdom surpassed that of ordinary people. Emperor Xuanzong summoned him to the upper floor of the pavilion, and Yang Guifei placed him on her knees, applying makeup and combing his hair. The emperor asked Liu, "You are the Imperial Scribe. How many characters are correctly written?" Liu replied, "All characters in the world are correct, except for the character '朋' (friend)." Yang Guifei then asked him to compose a poem about Wang Dainiang's performance on the pole. Liu immediately responded, "Before the pavilion, a hundred performances vie for novelty; only the long pole enters the divine realm with skill. Who would say that the silken robes are powerful, yet they still fear being too light and need more weight?" Emperor Xuanzong, along with Yang Guifei and the other consorts, laughed for a long time, and their laughter could be heard outside. As a result, he ordered ivory tablets and yellow ceremonial robes to be granted to Liu Yan.

The son of Yang Guozhong, Xuan, took the Mingjing examination, which was presided over by the Minister of Rites, Daxi Xun. Xun failed to pass him, and was about to disqualify him, but was afraid of Yang Guozhong and dared not set a specific time. The emperor was staying at Huaqing Palace, and Xun's son, Fu, was a local official. Xun hastily sent a messenger with a letter to Fu, instructing him to wait for Yang Guozhong and report the situation in detail. Fu arrived, and Yang Guozhong had just risen at the fifth watch, with torches lit at the gate, preparing to go to court. The carriages and horses were as numerous as in a market. Yang Guozhong was riding a horse, and Fu hurriedly entered and bowed before him in the candlelight. Yang Guozhong said his son must have been selected, and Fu smiled even more, looking very pleased. Fu then said, "I was ordered by my father to inform you that your son did not pass the examination, but I dared not disqualify him." Yang Guozhong turned and shouted, "What do I care if my son does not become wealthy and noble? How could I let him be disqualified by a mere name for the sake of a lowly official?" He did not listen and rode away. Fu was terrified and rushed to tell Xun, "Yang Guozhong relies on his power and behaves arrogantly, causing people's emotions to change in an instant. How can we argue about right and wrong? Because of this, Xuan was placed at the top rank. Later, Xun, who was a Minister of Rites, was promoted to Minister of Personnel, and he discussed this with his colleagues, still regretting his delay, and remarked that Xun's promotion was very rapid."

Xiao Yingshi passed the imperial examinations in the 23rd year of Kaiyuan, relying on his talent and being arrogant towards others, having no equal. He often carried a wine pot to seek pleasure in the countryside, and once rested at an inn, where he drank and recited poetry alone. Suddenly, a storm broke out, and an elderly man in purple clothing with a young boy sought shelter there. Xiao Yingshi saw the man's disheveled appearance and behaved rudely towards him. Shortly after, the wind stopped and the rain ceased, and suddenly, a carriage and horses arrived. The elderly man mounted his horse, shouted, and left. Xiao Yingshi looked around in surprise, and the attendants said it was the Minister of Personnel, Wang Shangshu, whose name was Qiu. Xiao Yingshi had often visited but had never met him, and was extremely surprised. The next day, he prepared a long letter and went to apologize. Qiu ordered him to be led to a pavilion and scolded him, saying, "Although I regret that we are not relatives, I should have disciplined you publicly." After a while, he said, "You rely on your literary reputation and behave so arrogantly. Is passing the exam all you care about?" Xiao Yingshi eventually became the Governor of Yangzhou and the Prefect of Runzhou. Wei Shen, a high-ranking official, sought a son-in-law but found none suitable despite their noble status and reputation. On New Year's Eve, while his wife and children were looking out from the city wall, they saw several people burying something in a garden. Wei Shen found this strange and sent officials to investigate.

The official returned and reported, saying, "What I saw was the residence of Caojun Pei Kuan." Shen invited Pei to come with him, and they both arrived. Shen recounted the reason for his visit, and Pei explained, "I have always sworn not to defile my home with bribes. Today, someone left a deer and left it there. Unable to deceive myself, I buried it in the back garden to preserve my integrity. I did not expect the governor to see it." Shen then descended the steps and said, "I have a daughter, and I wish to give her to the gentleman Pei." Pei bowed and thanked him before leaving. Upon returning, Shen told his wife, "I have always sought a good son-in-law, and now I have indeed found one." His wife asked, "Who is he?" Shen replied, "It is the person we saw on the city walls who buried the deer." The next day, Shen summoned Pei again, and the Wei family gathered to see him. Pei was wearing a green shirt, tall and thin. As he entered the house, the family laughed and called him a crane. Shen's wife wept in her curtain. Afterward, Shen told his wife, "If I love my daughter, I should let her marry a virtuous husband of a noble family. That man, pale as a gourd, is only fit to be a servant." Shen ultimately gave his daughter to Pei, and the Wei family lived together with Pei until old age. Their good fortune, longevity, and prosperity were unmatched among their relatives. Therefore, during the Kaiyuan and Tianbao eras, the Wei family was renowned among the old and respected families, with Pei Kuan being the foremost. Yao Yuanzong and Zhang Shuo were both chancellors, and they harbored suspicions and resentment toward each other, often attacking one another. Zhang bore this deeply. When Yao fell ill, he warned his sons, "Zhang the Chancellor and I have deep enmity. However, he is fond of luxury and especially enjoys fine clothes and decorations. After I die, since I was once his colleague, he will come to pay his respects. Display my lifetime's collection of fine clothes, precious items, and valuable objects on the front of the hall. If he does not look at them, quickly arrange the family affairs. There is no one like us in the family, so we have nothing to fear. You should then record the items and send them to Zhang the Chancellor, and also request a stele for his ancestral hall." After obtaining the text, he immediately wrote and submitted it, and then had the stone ground first to wait for it, and ordered it to be engraved. Zhang the Chancellor saw the matter late.

After several days, I will certainly regret it and use the excuse of revising the inscription as a reason to retract it. I will lead him to see the inscription and then inform him that it has been reported to the emperor. After Yao died, Zhang Guo arrived and observed the playthings and clothing of Yao's children, and Yao's sons and daughters all followed his instructions. Within a few days, the text was completed, detailing everything comprehensively. Here is a brief summary: Eight pillars supporting the sky, high and bright, the four seasons completing the year, the work of nurturing and destroying. After a few more days, Zhang Guo sent someone to retrieve the original text, claiming it was incomplete and needed further revision. Yao's children led the messenger to see the stone tablet and informed him that it had been submitted to the emperor. The messenger returned and expressed regret, saying, "Even dead Yao Cheng can still outsmart living Zhang Shuo. I now know how far inferior my talent is." Li Shi, who was both wealthy and powerful, often placed several tripods in front of him to serve food. One day, the tripods in the courtyard leaped out and fought each other. The servants told Li Shi, who went to the place, poured wine, and took an oath, but the fight did not stop, and the ears and feet of the tripods fell off. The next day, Li Shi was dismissed from his position as chancellor and appointed as the Grand Tutor of the Crown Prince. People knew that his misfortune had not yet ended. Soon, he was implicated by Li Linfu and demoted to the governor of Yichun. His son, Zha, who was a Junior Secretary of the Guards, was also demoted to the Deputy Governor of Balin Prefecture. Li Shi arrived at the prefecture and died within a few months. People believed that Li Linfu had forced him to die. Zha then brought his father's body to the capital, but Li Linfu was still angry and had someone falsely accuse him in the Henan Prefecture, leading to his execution by beating. Li Shi enjoyed drinking and, after leaving the court, would quickly gather friends and relatives to talk and write poetry, which Li Linfu was not aware of. When Li Shi was in his prime as chancellor, he once wrote a poem: "At the red gate, guests never lack; friends and relatives freely come and go. Today, I am over fifty; if I don't drink, what else can I do?" After being dismissed from his position, he wrote another poem: "Initially, I resigned as chancellor to avoid the talented. I enjoy the company of the wise and hold my cup. Ask the guests at my door: how many are there today?" When he died, it was not his fault, and people mourned his unjust fate.

Li Linfu's residence also experienced frequent strange and ominous phenomena; there was a large fire in the northern and southern corners of the moat, and sometimes there were children carrying fire in and out. Li Linfu was displeased by these events and reported to the emperor, requesting the construction of the Jiaoyou Pavilion at the site. When Li Linfu was ill, he got up early to go to court and ordered his attendants to bring his bag containing the usual list of important matters. Suddenly, he felt that the bag was unusually heavy. Upon opening it, two rats emerged and, upon being thrown to the ground, transformed into a large, fierce black dog that looked up at Li Linfu with its eyes wide open and teeth bared. He ordered someone to shoot it, and after a loud noise, the dog disappeared. Li Linfu was displeased and claimed illness, not attending court. On that day, he fell ill and died within a month. Empress Wu Zetian once gathered all the grandsons of the royal family and observed their play, giving them precious items from the Western countries to see how they would react. Most of them rushed to grab the items, but only Emperor Xuanzong sat calmly without moving. She was very surprised and patted his back, saying, "This child will be the future emperor of a peaceful era." She then ordered the retrieval of the Jade Dragon and gave it to him. The Jade Dragon was a treasure obtained by Emperor Taizong in the JinYang Palace, which Empress Wu Zetian had kept in her wardrobe and later gave to the newborn Emperor Gaozong on the third day after his birth, wrapped in red silk along with the Jade Dragon. It was later kept in the imperial treasury, though small in size, it was warm, smooth, and exquisitely crafted, not found in the world. When Emperor Xuanzong ascended the throne, every time there was a heavy rain in the capital, he would...

During the Kaiyuan period, there was a severe drought in the three regions (三辅). The emperor sincerely prayed for rain, and soon after, clouds gathered and rain began to fall. The emperor then secretly threw something into the Dragon Pool in the southern palace. Soon after, clouds gathered and rain began to fall. When the emperor was on his way to Shu (West Sichuan), he stopped by the Wei River and his entourage was about to cross. Some of the court attendants bathed or played by the river and found something in the sand. The emperor was surprised and moved to tears upon seeing the object, identifying it as a precious jade dragon he once owned. After this, the jade dragon emitted a radiant light at night, illuminating the room. After returning to the capital, a eunuch stole the jade dragon and gave it to Li Fuguo. Li Fuguo kept the jade dragon in a cabinet, but it disappeared when he was about to fall from power. Wang Chen's son, Zhuang, served as a junior official and entertained the emperor with cockfighting. Li Linfu held power, and his son, Xiu, also served in the imperial court. Xiu was often insulted by Zhuang but dared not speak up. One day, Zhuang led his followers to Prince Wang Yao's private residence, where Yao bowed respectfully. Zhuang shot an arrow at Yao's hat, breaking a jade hairpin, and found it amusing. They then served wine and music, with Princess Yongmu personally attending. The emperor's anger was so great that no one dared defy him, especially since their lives depended on Zhuang.

Wang Maozhong, originally from Goryeo, served diligently by the side of Li Yide, and the emperor loved them all. Each time attending banquets, they sat on the same couch as Jiang Jiao in front of the emperor. Later, they became arrogant and relied on their old connections, becoming increasingly lawless, but the emperor often tolerated them. Each time sending eunuchs to inquire, if Wang Maozhong was slightly dissatisfied, he would indulge in insulting them, and then send them back. Gao Lishi and Yang Sijue deeply resented him but never dared to speak to the emperor. On the third day after Wang Maozhong's wife gave birth, the emperor ordered Gao Lishi to bestow wine, food, gold, and silk upon her, which were very generous, and also appointed his son as a fifth-rank official. After Gao Lishi returned, the emperor asked, "Is Wang Maozhong happy? What more does he want to say?" Gao Lishi replied, "He took out his son to show me, and I looked at him closely in the cradle, saying, 'This child surely deserves a third-rank official position.'" The emperor became very angry and said, "When I exterminated the Wei family, this traitor still hesitated and avoided involvement; I never mentioned it. Now he dares to resent me because of his newborn child?" From then on, the emperor's favor and affection waned. After ascending the throne during the Tianbao era, the emperor had been on the throne for fifteen years, and only four people had been granted the rank of Grand Commandant. They were the father-in-law Wang Renjiao, Yao Chong, Song Jing, and Wang Maozhong.

**The First Volume of Records of the Ming Emperor** Imperial Library Catalogue **The Second Volume of Records of the Ming Emperor** Tang Dynasty Zheng Chuhui Compiled **Zhang Jiuling, while serving as chancellor, was known for his honest and upright nature. After Emperor Xuanzong had been on the throne for many years and began to neglect state affairs, Zhang Jiuling would always speak frankly about the emperor's strengths and weaknesses whenever he met with the emperor. Li Linfu, who was serving in the same capacity at the time, heard that the emperor had secretly decided to harm Zhang Jiuling. When the emperor wanted to appoint Niu Xianke as the governor of Shuofang and grant him a real fief, Zhang Jiuling argued that it was inappropriate, which greatly displeased the emperor. One day, Li Linfu requested an audience and repeatedly accused Zhang Jiuling of harboring resentment. It was autumn at the time, and the emperor ordered Gao Lishi to present a white feather fan to Zhang Jiuling, intending to convey a message. Zhang Jiuling, feeling anxious, composed an essay and presented it to the emperor. He also wrote a poem about returning swallows to give to Li Linfu. The poem read: "Seabirds, how small and humble, they come in spring, yet they know not the mud and silt. They see only the jade hall open, entering the embroidered room in pairs, and returning to the splendid hall many times. They have no desire to compete with others, so do not suspect them of being eagles or falcons." Li Linfu, upon reading the poem, understood that Zhang Jiuling would be forced to resign and his anger subsided somewhat. On the day Zhang Jiuling and Pei Yaoqing were dismissed from their posts, they walked from the Ministry of State Affairs to the Moonlight Gate, where they were to take their positions. Both men bowed humbly, while Li Linfu stood in the middle, speaking confidently and authoritatively. Onlookers whispered that it was like an eagle carrying two falcons. Soon after, the emperor issued an edict appointing Zhang Jiuling and Pei Yaoqing as the left and right vice-premiers, respectively, and removing them from their roles in government. Li Linfu, upon seeing the edict, flew into a rage and said:**

Are they to serve as the Left and Right Chancellors? The two hastened to take their proper positions. Lin Fu watched them go, and the officials below could not help but tremble with fear. Emperor Xuanzong held a grand banquet at the Qinxing Pavilion, where the streets were cleared of residents. After the banquet, the emperor still lowered his curtains to watch the Secretary of War Lu Xuan, who was reputed for his literary refinement and had a clear and pure demeanor. The emperor, seeing Lu Xuan, could not help but watch him go, and asked those around him, "Who is this near servant?" They replied with Lu Xuan's name and title. The emperor immediately praised his refined and dignified demeanor. At that time, Lin Fu was holding power and jealous of talent, and the emperor's favored attendants never failed to lavish him with gold and silk as bribes. Thus, Lin Fu knew everything about the emperor's movements. The next day, Lin Fu summoned Lu Xuan's sons and said, "Your esteemed father is renowned for his high moral standing. Now, the emperor has entrusted him with the southern provinces. Is it possible?" If he dreads the distance, he should request retirement; otherwise, to remain as a guest lecturer and divide duties in eastern Luoyang is also a generous command for the wise. Go back and tell us whether the proposal is acceptable. Thus, Lu Xuan requested the position of guest lecturer. Lin Fu feared disappointing public opinion and appointed him as the governor of Huazhou. Within a month, he accused him of illness and poor governance, and appointed him as an external member of the Imperial Household Department.

Emperor Xuanzong of Tang held a grand feast at the Wu Feng Lou in Eastern Luoyang. He ordered officials from within a 300-li radius to bring their musical ensembles to the capital. Some suggested that he compare their performances and reward or punish accordingly. At the time, the governor of Henan Prefecture ordered hundreds of musicians to perform on vehicles adorned with tiger skins and shaped like rhinos and elephants, which amazed the onlookers. Meanwhile, Yuan Lushan sent dozens of musicians to sing and dance together, performing the music of Lu Shan. Upon hearing this, Emperor Xuanzong was impressed and inquired about the lyrics, sighing and saying, "These are the words of a wise man." Later, the emperor told his prime ministers, "Are the people of Henan Prefecture suffering in the mud and fire?" He quickly ordered Yuan Lushan to return and granted him an honorary position. Every time a grand feast was set up, the emperor would personally attend the Qin Zheng Lou. The Jin Wu and the four military divisions would line up their weapons and flags, all wearing golden armor and embroidered robes. The Tai Chang would arrange the music, and the Wei Wei would set up the curtains behind which the leaders of various ethnic groups would dine. The imperial music and dance troupe would perform on traditional vehicles and boats, with acrobats balancing on poles and tightropes, sword fighting, and ball juggling. There were also wrestling matches, horse races, and cockfights. The emperor would also order hundreds of palace women to perform, dressed in pearls and jade, with embroidered robes, striking drums and singing the *Ba Zhen Le* (Battle Array Music), *Tai Ping Le* (Peaceful Music), and *Shang Yuan Le* (Lantern Festival Music). Elephants and rhinos were also brought in, sometimes bowing and dancing in time with the music. Every night during the Lantern Festival, the emperor would personally attend the Qin Zheng Lou to watch the performances. Nobles and officials would set up viewing platforms, and palace women would perform dances and songs to entertain the guests.

During the early Tang Dynasty under Emperor Xuanzong, the musicians Li Guonian, Pengnian, and Heyen, brothers all, were renowned for their exceptional talent and fame. Pengnian was skilled in dance, while Guonian and Heyen excelled in singing, particularly in the style of Wei and Shu. They were especially favored by the emperor and constructed a luxurious residence in the Eastern Capital that surpassed even the mansions of dukes and marquises. This residence, located in Tongyuan Lane, was the most splendid in the capital. [Today, the residence has been moved to a villa outside the Gate of the Anvil, named Green Field Hall.] Afterward, Guonian traveled to the south, where he would often sing at auspicious occasions and beautiful gatherings. Those who heard him in the audience would often cover their faces and cry, stopping their drinking. Du Fu, a famous Tang Dynasty poet, once wrote a poem about them, saying, "I often saw them in the residence of Prince Qi, and heard them many times in the residence of Cui Jiu. Just when the southern region was in good weather, I encountered them again during the time of falling flowers." The residence of Cui Jiu, a high-ranking official, was known as the Hall of the Chamberlain of the Palace and the Hall of the Secretary of the Secretariat. During the early Tang Dynasty, the Guanzhong region suffered a severe drought, and the capital was particularly lacking in rain. The emperor urgently ordered the ministers to pray to the mountains and rivers, but there was no response. The emperor then ordered the construction of a new hall at the Dragon Pool. He summoned Feng Shaozheng, the Supervisor of the Imperial Workshops, and instructed him to paint a dragon on each of the four walls. Feng began by painting the dragons in a lifelike manner, as if they were about to leap out of the wall. Halfway through the painting, clouds and mist began to rise, and the emperor and his attendants, watching from below, saw that the scales of the dragons were wet. Before the painting was complete, a white vapor emerged from the eaves, and the dragons appeared to be entering and exiting the pool. Waves and tides surged, and thunder and lightning followed. Hundreds of attendants saw a white dragon rising from the waves, ascending on a cloud. Soon, the sky darkened, and rain poured down, filling the entire region with a bountiful downpour within a day.

During the An Lushan Rebellion, when An Lushan captured Chang'an and Luoyang, Wang Wei, Zheng Fang, and Zhang Jun were all in the court of the rebels. After the rebels were defeated, they were all imprisoned in the former residence of Yang Guozhong. Cui Yuan, the chancellor, then summoned them to his private residence and ordered them to paint several famous paintings on the walls. At that time, everyone hoped for Cui Yuan's help because of his high rank and merit. Therefore, they thought deeply and painted with great skill, and all received lenient treatment. Even when they were demoted, they were sent to good places. When Zhang Yue was exiled to Yue Prefecture, he was very unhappy. The officials at the time, due to Zhang Yue's sharp wit and strategic insight, were against him. Su Ting was about to be promoted, and Zhang Yue had a good relationship with him. Zhang Yue wrote a poem in praise of five gentlemen and sent it to Su Ting. He instructed his messenger to wait until near dusk on the day of mourning to deliver it. When the messenger arrived, he delivered the letter to Su Ting's residence on the day of mourning. It had been raining for many days, and near dusk, many mourners arrived. Zhang Yue was a former colleague of Su Ting's father. Su Ting read the poem and cried, unable to control his emotions. The next day, he submitted a memorial to the emperor, praising Zhang Yue's loyalty, integrity, and contributions to stabilizing the state, and arguing that he should not be kept in a remote place. The emperor then sent a letter of commendation, and soon promoted Zhang Yue to the position of Prefectural Governor of Jingzhou. Lu Xiangxian, Wei Sili, Zhang Tingui, and Jia Zeng were all demoted for a long time and were eventually reinstated. Su Ting always treated Zhang Yue with great respect because he was a close friend of Zhang Yue's father, and Zhang Yue highly valued Su Ting's talent and character. Emperor Xuanzong visited the Huaqing Palace and ordered the construction of a new hot spring pool.

In Fanyang, a grand and strict model of An Lushan was constructed, carved from white and black jade into fish, dragons, geese, and wild ducks, as well as stone bridges and stone lotus flowers, presented with exquisite skill that seemed not human work. The emperor was greatly pleased and ordered them to be displayed in the hot springs. He also had stone bridges span the hot springs, with lotus flowers barely emerging from the water's edge. When the emperor visited the Huaqing Palace and was about to enter, the fish, dragons, geese, and wild ducks seemed to flap their wings and raise their scales, appearing as if they were about to fly. The emperor was greatly alarmed and hastily ordered them to be removed, but the lotus flowers remained. He also had several dozen long hot spring houses built, surrounded and paved with patterned stones, and silver-inlaid lacquer boats and white fragrant wood boats placed within, all adorned with pearls and jade. Additionally, he had green jade and clove piled up to form mountains, resembling the mythical islands of Yingzhou and Fangzhang. When the emperor was about to visit the Huaqing Palace, the sisters of the Consort Yang competed in their carriages and attire. One carriage was decorated with gold, jade, and pearls, and the cost of one carriage was no less than several hundred thousand strings of cash. However, it was so heavy that the oxen could not pull it.

Thereupon, the report was submitted to the emperor again, requesting that each person ride a horse. Consequently, they competed to buy famous horses, decorating them with gold bits and embroidered saddlecloths. They gathered at the residence of Guo Zhong, intending to enter the imperial palace together. The spectacle was bright and shining, with spectators as dense as a wall. From Guo Zhong's residence to the southeast corner of the city, the scene was chaotic, with servants and drivers managing the horses and carriages. Guo Zhong was sitting with guests at the gate, pointing and saying to the guests, "Our family rose from humble beginnings, due to the kinship through the imperial harem, we have reached this point. I do not know where to park my carriage, thinking that I cannot achieve a good reputation, but I will take pleasure in wealth and status." Thus, a state of arrogance, luxury, and overstepping one's bounds emerged, obscuring the way of maintaining fullness and balance. Princess Taiping's jade leaf crown, Lady Guo's night-light pillow, and Yang Guozhong's chain-link canopy were all rare treasures of the era, their value cannot be calculated. Yang Guifei's sister, known as Lady Guo, enjoyed grace and favor at one time, greatly renovating her residences, whose grandeur was unmatched. The residence formerly belonging to Wei Sili, Wei's sons were just at noon.

Resting in the hall and corridors, he suddenly saw a woman dressed in a yellow silk robe and cape, descending from a sedan chair. With dozens of maidservants speaking and laughing as usual, she said to the sons of Wei, "I hear this house is for sale. What is the price?" The sons of Wei stepped down from the steps and replied, "This is our ancestral home, which we have not yet been able to give up." Before she finished speaking, hundreds of workers began to demolish the east and west wings, removing the tiles and wood. The sons of Wei then led their household servants, carrying away their musical instruments and books and abandoning them on the road, and gave the sons of Wei a few acres of vacant land, with no compensation for the house. After the central hall in Guo was completed, craftsmen were called in to plaster and smooth the walls. Two million coins were given to pay the value, and three measures of golden cups and jade were given as a reward. Later, a strong wind blew down tiles and left them on the hall, but when they looked, there was hardly any damage. After removing the tiles to inspect, they found that the tiles were supported by wooden frames, and the construction was very meticulous, all of this kind. The state of Guo often entered the inner palace, riding a gray horse and having a low-ranking eunuch drive it. The gray horse was strong and vigorous, and the eunuch was elegant and handsome, both outstanding at the time.

Zhang Guo, who frequently traveled between the regions of Hezhōu, Tiāoshān, Fén, and Jìn, was said by people of the time to possess secret techniques for longevity. The elders recounted that they had seen him as a child, and he claimed to be hundreds of years old. Emperors Taizong and Gaozong of Tang repeatedly summoned him, but he did not respond. Empress Wu Zetian summoned him from the mountains and he pretended to die in front of the Dùnǚ Temple during a time of great heat. In a short while, his body rotted and produced maggots, and Empress Wu Zetian believed he had died. Later, someone saw him again in the mountains of Hezhōu. Zhang Guo rode a white donkey that could travel tens of thousands of miles in a day. When he rested, he folded the donkey, which was as thin as paper, and placed it in a box for towels. When he wanted to ride, he sprayed water on it, and it turned back into a donkey. In the 23rd year of the Kaiyuan era (735 AD), Emperor Xuanzong sent the Interpreter Secretary Pei Wu to Hezhōu to welcome him. Zhang Guo pretended to die before Pei Wu. Pei Wu burned incense and prayed to convey the emperor's desire to seek the Dao. In a short while, Zhang Guo gradually revived. Pei Wu dared not press him and hurried back to report. Emperor Xuanzong then ordered the Secretary of the Ministry of Rites Xu Qiao to carry the imperial edict to welcome him. Zhang Guo followed Xu Qiao to the Eastern Capital, where he was settled in the Jixian Academy. He entered the palace in a sedan chair and was treated with great respect. Emperor Xuanzong then calmly said, "Master, who has attained the Dao, why do you have such aged teeth and hair?" Zhang Guo replied.

In old age, lacking any method or skill to rely on, he caused himself to be in such a state, which is truly shameful. Now, if we were to completely remove it, would that not be better? In front of the emperor, he pulled out his own hair and knocked out his teeth, causing blood to flow from his mouth. Emperor Xuanzong was greatly surprised and said, "Master, please rest for a short while before we speak again." After a short while, he was summoned again, with black hair and white teeth, looking better than in his prime. One day, Secretariat Supervisor Wang Jiongzi and Imperial Sacrificial Rites Deputy Minister Xiao Hua once visited him together. At that time, Emperor Xuanzong wanted to marry Princess Yuzhen to him, but he did not know about it. Suddenly, he laughed and said to the two men, "Marriage to a princess is very frightening." Wang Jiongzi and Xiao Hua looked at each other, not understanding what he meant. Soon after, a middle-ranking official arrived and told Guo, "The emperor, because Princess Yuzhen had a love for the Dao since her youth, wanted to marry her to you." Guo laughed loudly and did not accept the imperial decree. The two men then realized the meaning of his earlier words. At that time, many officials visited him, and when they asked about matters outside the ordinary, he always gave evasive answers, often saying he was a person from the year Bingzi during the reign of Emperor Yao, which no one could verify. He also said that during Emperor Yao's reign, he served as a minister and was skilled in fetal breathing, able to go without food for several days, sometimes only drinking fine wine and taking three yellow pills. Emperor Xuanzong invited him to the palace and offered him wine, but he declined, saying he was a mountain dweller.

The legendary Daoist sage Zhang Guo could not drink more than two sheng (a unit of volume), but he had a disciple who could drink up to one dou (a larger unit of volume). Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, upon hearing this, was delighted and ordered him to be summoned. Soon, a young Taoist priest flew down from the eaves of the palace, aged about sixteen or seventeen, with a beautiful appearance and a refined, calm demeanor. He bowed respectfully to the emperor and spoke in a clear and composed manner. The emperor invited him to sit, but Zhang Guo said, “Your disciple should stand beside you and is not yet worthy of sitting.” The emperor looked at him with increasing pleasure and insisted on offering him wine. Zhang Guo drank up to one dou without hesitation. He then declined, saying, “I cannot accept more. Drinking too much will lead to some loss, and it will only cause the emperor to smile.” The emperor pressed him further, and suddenly, a crown emerged from his head, fell to the ground, and transformed into a wine vessel. The emperor and his attendants were both surprised and amused. By the time they looked again, the Taoist priest had vanished, leaving only a golden wine vessel on the ground. When they overturned it, they found a seal inside, which turned out to be the same as the one in the Imperial Academy of Literature. Zhang Guo performed numerous magical feats that were too many to record. There was a person named Guī Yèguāng who was skilled at seeing ghosts. Emperor Xuanzong often invited Zhang Guo to sit in front of him and ordered Guī Yèguāng to look for him. Guī Yèguāng approached the emperor and reported, “I do not know where Master Zhang Guo is; I wish to inspect the area.” However, Zhang Guo had been standing in front of the emperor for a long time, and Guī Yèguāng could not see him. There was also a person named Xíng Hépú.

In arithmetic, whenever Zhang Guo looked at a person, he would lay out counting rods in front of him. In a short while, he could detail their names, surnames, prosperity, adversity, good and evil, and whether they would live long or die young. For all the thousands of calculations he made, he never failed to analyze even the smallest details. Emperor Xuanzong found this remarkable and had been observing him for a long time. When he commanded Zhang Guo to calculate, Zhang Guo moved the rods for a while, but his mind grew weary and his spirit depressed, and he could not determine the person's age or birth year. Emperor Xuanzong then said to the eunuch Gao Lishi, "I have heard that immortals are not affected by cold and heat, and external things do not disturb them. Now, Zhang Guo is an expert in calculation, but no one can determine his age, and no one can see his appearance. Could it be that he is a true immortal? However, I have also heard that those who drink the same wine, *Jinzhen*, will surely die. If he is not an immortal, the wine will surely harm him. We can test him with it." It happened that there was a heavy snowfall, and the weather was extremely cold. Emperor Xuanzong ordered the *Jinzhen* wine to be brought in and presented to Zhang Guo. Zhang Guo then drank it all, finishing three cups. He appeared slightly drunk and said to those around him, "This wine is not of good taste." He then lay down and fell asleep. After a meal's time, he woke up and suddenly looked in the mirror, seeing that his teeth were all spotted and blackened. He immediately ordered his servant to take an iron scepter and strike his teeth, which fell out one by one and were collected in his waistband. Slowly undressing, he took out a dose of medicine, which was slightly red and glossy.

In the tooth cavity, the treatment was applied, and then he slept for a long time. Upon waking, he looked again in the mirror and saw that his teeth had grown back, firmer and brighter than before. Emperor Xuanzong just began to believe in the mysterious and supernatural nature of the event. He said to the strong man, "Could this not be a true immortal?" Then he issued an edict saying, "Zhang Guo, the Master of Profound Tao from Hengzhou, is one who wanders outside the world. He has traces of high moral character, and his mind enters the profound and mysterious. He has long been mixed with the mundane world but remains unaffected by it. Responding to the call, he went to the capital, and no one knows his exact age. He is called a person from the time of Fuxi, and when asked about the core principles of Taoism, he has exhausted all means to reach the ultimate. Now, he is about to perform court rituals, and I will issue a favorable command. He can be granted the title of Silver and Blue Light禄大夫 and bestowed the title of Master of Profound Tao." Not long after, Emperor Xuanzong hunted in Xianyang and caught a large deer, which was slightly unusual. The cook was preparing the food when Zhang Guo saw the deer and said, "This is an immortal deer, having already reached a thousand years old." In the past, during the fifth year of the Yuan Shou era of Emperor Wu of Han, he once attended the emperor's hunting in Shanglin Park, where the deer was caught alive at that time. Afterwards, the deer was released. Emperor Xuanzong said, "There are many deer. Time passes and generations change. How can it not have been caught by hunters?" Zhang Guo said, "When Emperor Wu released the deer, he marked it with a bronze plate under its left horn." The emperor then ordered the verification of the mark. Zhang Guo found a bronze plate about two inches in size, but the writing on it was faded.

In a secluded setting, Emperor Xuanzong asked Taoist master Guo, "What is the year of Yuanshuo in the sexagenary cycle, and how many years have passed since then?" Guo replied, "This year is Guihai, when Emperor Wu of Han began to construct the Kunming Pool. Now it is Ji Xu, and 852 years have passed." Emperor Xuanzong then ordered the historians to verify the long-term calendar, and it matched without error. The emperor was amazed by this. At that time, there was also a Taoist named Ye Fashan, who was skilled in various arts. Emperor Xuanzong asked, "Who is Guo?" Ye Fashan replied, "I know him, but if I reveal his identity, I will die. Therefore, I dare not speak. If Your Majesty removes your hat and shoes and pleads for my life, I will be saved." The emperor agreed, and Ye Fashan said, "This is a spirit from the primordial chaos, a white bat demon." As soon as he finished speaking, Guo's seven orifices began to bleed, and he collapsed and lay motionless on the ground. Emperor Xuanzong rushed to Guo's place, removed his hat and shoes, and confessed his sins. Guo calmly said, "This child has too many words; if I punish him, it might disrupt the harmony of the world." The emperor then pleaded with Guo for a long time. Guo sprinkled water on his face, and Ye Fashan immediately came back to life. Later, Guo repeatedly claimed to be old and ill, requesting to return to Hengzhou. The emperor granted him official transportation to Hengzhou. In the early years of the Tianbao era, Emperor Xuanzong again sent someone to summon Guo. Upon hearing this, Guo suddenly died. His disciples buried him, but when they opened the coffin later, it was empty.

Li Xiazhou was a Daoist adept with considerable mystical abilities. During the early Tang Dynasty under Emperor Xuanzong, he was once summoned to the inner palace but later requested to leave and reside at the Xuan Dao Temple. The Tang prime minister Li Linfu once visited him. Li Xiazhou said to him, "If you remain alive, your family will prosper; if you die, your family will perish." Li Linfu knelt and wept, requesting him to save him. Li Xiazhou laughed and did not respond, saying, "I was just joking." At the end of the Tianbao era, An Lushan became arrogant and rebellious, causing worry both far and near, yet the emperor's intentions remained unawakened. One day, Li Xiazhou had already left, and no one knew where he went. However, he inscribed several poems on the wall of his former residence, foretelling An Lushan's usurpation and the emperor's flight to Sichuan. At the time, no one understood the meaning of these poems, but later they were verified. The last poem reads: "All the people of Yan left, Horses did not return from Hangu Pass, Encountering a ghost at the foot of the mountain, A ring tied with a silk robe. All the people of Yan left, An Lushan gathered all the troops of You and Jizhou to rise up. Horses did not return from Hangu Pass, This refers to Gao Xianzhi's defeat at Tongguan, where not a single horse returned. Encountering a ghost at the foot of the mountain, This refers to the Ma Wei station in Sichuan. A ring tied with a silk robe, This refers to the concubine Yang Guifei, whose nickname was Yuhuan. At Ma Wei, Gao Lishi strangled her with a silk cloth. All that he foresaw was of this kind."

During the Kaiyuan era, the court selected officials with great care, and cultural and political institutions flourished. Talented individuals were often assigned to distant or less prestigious positions, even important roles were sometimes given to officials who were considered surplus or less capable, which was seen as a demotion. Ban Jingqian, an inspector of Yangzhou, was promoted to a lesser position in the capital. On his way, he passed through Daliang, where Ni Ruoshui, the governor, held a grand farewell banquet for him. After the banquet, Ban Jingqian boarded his boat. Ni Ruoshui, watching the dust of his departing carriage, told his subordinates, "Ban Gong's departure is no different from ascending to the heavens. To serve as his escort would be a great honor." He remained silent for a long time before finally returning to the capital. Later, he composed a poem to express his feelings, which was well-received by the court. Emperor Xuanzong highly valued Su Ting and intended to make him a chancellor, treating him differently from other officials. The day before he was to be appointed, Emperor Xuanzong kept the matter secret, not wanting anyone to know. Late at night, he ordered a draft of the appointment decree to be prepared and summoned Xiao Song, a high-ranking official, to draft it. Upon Xiao Song's arrival, Emperor Xuanzong gave him Su Ting's name and instructed him to draft the decree. After it was completed, the words read, "The nation's treasure." Emperor Xuanzong read it several times and told Xiao Song, "Ting is the son of a treasure. I do not want to mention his father's name. Please revise it." He then ordered the screen in his tent to be removed. Xiao Song, feeling ashamed and fearful, sweated profusely and could not write for a long time. Emperor Xuanzong, seeing Xiao Song's effort, moved forward to read the draft, making only one change, "The nation's precious gem," and said no more. After Xiao Song left, Emperor Xuanzong threw the draft to the ground, saying, "It is hollow inside." [Xiao Song was tall with a long beard, which is why he was given this name.] Those around him laughed, and upon hearing this, Emperor Xuanzong quickly covered their mouths, saying, "Although Xiao Song's talents are not outstanding, the dignity of a minister is unparalleled. My previous words were just a joke. His silent insight and discernment are indeed remarkable."

Emperor Xuanzong appointed Yu Wenrong as the Commissioner for Land Surveying. Yu Wenrong acted arrogantly and recklessly, and those who did not align with him were inevitably accused falsely. He secretly submitted a report, claiming that Lu Congyuan widely acquired farmland, owning hundreds of acres. The emperor had always been highly regarded and trusted Lu Congyuan, relying on him as chancellor several times, and his family prestige and official connections were at their peak during that time. Therefore, the emperor did not openly speak of his crimes but referred to Lu Congyuan as "the old man with much land." When young, Lu Congyuan lived in Xiangzhou, repeatedly passed the imperial examinations, and was appointed as a county magistrate in Xia County. He served as a deputy minister of the Ministry of Personnel for ten years before becoming a minister, and rose from an assistant position in the Ministry of Personnel to deputy minister in just seven months. --- **Translation of the Second Part:** After being exiled to the Dan Valley in Wushan, Gao Lishi found that although there were many wild chives, people did not eat them. Gao Lishi was moved by this and wrote a poem to express his feelings. He sold the chives in the two capitals by the pound, but in the Wuxi region, no one would buy them. Although the land was fertile, the flavor of the chives remained unchanged. Later, when a general amnesty was granted, Gao Lishi returned to Wuxi. On the way, he encountered a guard from the imperial army who had been exiled to the south for a crime. Stopping to visit an old acquaintance, Gao Lishi learned that the former emperor had passed away. Looking northward, Gao Lishi wept and vomited blood, dying from grief. ---

At the end of the Tianbao era, the rebels captured both capitals, Chang'an and Luoyang, and looted the officials, palace attendants, musicians, and horsemen. Whenever they captured several hundred people, they escorted them to Luoyang with strict military protection. Some even managed to escape into the mountains, but the rebels eventually captured and coerced them, bestowing official titles upon them. An Lushan, in particular, showed great interest in the musicians, and he sought them out diligently. Within a few days, he captured several hundred Pear Garden disciples. The rebels then gathered in a grand feast at the Ningbi Pool, where dozens of false officials were present, and the imperial treasures were displayed before and behind them. As the music began, the old Pear Garden musicians could not help but weep, and they wept together. The rebels, brandishing their weapons, threatened them, but the musicians were so overwhelmed with grief that they could not hold back their tears. One of the musicians, Le Haiqing, threw his musical instruments to the ground and wept bitterly towards the west. The rebel party then tied Le Haiqing to the steed-play pavilion and dismembered him in front of the crowd. Those who heard of it were all deeply saddened. At the time, Wang Wei was held captive in the Pudiji Temple and, upon hearing of the events, composed a poem: "Ten thousand households grieve, giving rise to wild smoke. When will the officials return to court? Autumn elm leaves fall in the empty palace, while music is played at the Ningbi Pool." In the Tianbao era of the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Sun Zhengsheng who was deeply versed in Daoist arts. Emperor Xuanzong summoned him to the capital. Sun Zhengsheng was skilled in stacking stones, piling eggs, and creating figures of people and horses from grass, which he could ride and gallop east and west. Princess Yuzhen was particularly fond of his arts and often summoned him to the palace to demonstrate his skills. When the An Lushan Rebellion broke out, Sun Zhengsheng's whereabouts became unknown. During the Kaiyuan era of the Tang Dynasty, there was a monk named Yi Fu from Shangdang. He practiced the monastic life with great diligence and had a dignified and pure appearance. Scholars, officials, and common people all flocked to him. He once accompanied the emperor to the Eastern Capital, and wherever he went, the people were drawn to him, and he received immense offerings, which he left behind when he departed. One day, he called his disciples and announced that he was about to die. The Secretariat Minister Zhang Jun, the Vice-Chancellor Yan Tingzhi, and the Minister of Justice Fang were all deeply affected by his passing.

### Translation Wang Gua, the Vice Minister of Rites, and Wei Zhi, who was always respectfully visited by Wang, also came together on that day. Yi Fu ascended the seat and taught the disciples, saying, "I will pass away on this day, so it is time to part ways." After a while, Zhang said to Fang, "For many years, I have taken elixirs of immortality, and I have never attended a funeral." After he finished speaking, Zhang quietly left. Yi Fu suddenly said to Fang, "I have been with Zhang Gong for several years now. Zhang has committed a grave offense, and his reputation and integrity have been compromised. If he had completed the ritual, he could have avoided this calamity. Alas!" He then took Fang's hand and said, "You will surely become a prominent minister in the future. You must strive." After he finished speaking, he passed away. When the An Lushan Rebellion broke out, Zhang Jun was captured by the rebels and appointed to a false position, while Fang Guan remained loyal to both the old and new dynasties, ultimately establishing a noble character. The monk Yi Xing, whose family name was Zhang and whose native place was Juxu, had the given name Suo. When the Emperor Xuanzong of Tang summoned him, he asked, "What are your abilities?" Yi Xing replied, "I am only good at memorizing and reading." The Emperor then ordered the palace to fetch the register of palace women and showed it to him. After thoroughly reviewing it, Yi Xing recited it perfectly from memory, as if he had read it before. After reading several pages, the Emperor could not help but get off his throne, bowing to him and calling him "Holy One." Previously, Yi Xing had followed the Buddhist teachings and studied under Pu Ji at Mount Song. Once, the master held a feast at the temple, and monks from all over the region, living hundreds of miles away, all arrived on time, gathering over a thousand people. At that time, there was a man named Lu Hong, who was highly learned and reclusive, living on Mount Song. He was invited to write an article to praise the event. On the day of the feast, Lu Hong brought his article to the temple. The master handed it to him and placed it on the table. As the bells and drums began to sound, Lu Hong asked Pu Ji, "I have written several thousand words, and the characters are obscure and the language strange. Why don't we select a few of the more intelligent monks to personally teach them?" The master then ordered Yi Xing to be summoned. When Yi Xing arrived, he spread out the paper, smiled, and read it in one glance, then returned it to the master.

A large bird was placed on a table, and its loose feathers were noticed, leading to a sense of surprise. Soon after, a group of monks gathered in the hall. Yixing, with a gesture of raising his sleeves, entered and spoke with a strong voice, ensuring nothing was forgotten. The bird was surprised and astonished for a long time, and Yixing said to him, "It is not within your power to teach this. You should be allowed to travel and study." Yixing, having mastered the *Great Derivation*, began to seek out teachers, traveling far and wide. He once visited the *Guqing Temple* in Tiantai, where he saw an ancient courtyard with dozens of pine trees and a stream flowing by the gate. Yixing stood between the door and the screen, hearing the sound of monks arranging calculations in the courtyard, with a rustling sound. Soon, one of the monks said to his disciples, "Today, a student will come to learn our algorithm, and it seems no one has guided them yet." He then removed one of the calculations and said, "The water in front of the temple has changed direction and is now flowing westward; the student will arrive." Yixing, upon hearing this, entered the temple, bowed respectfully, and asked for the teachings. The monks taught him all their methods, and the water in front of the temple, which used to flow eastward, suddenly changed direction and flowed westward. Xing Hepu once said to Yin Yin, "Is Yixing a sage? In the Han Dynasty, Luo Xiaohong created the *Great Derivation Calendar*, and it is said that after eight hundred years, a sage would appear."

This year, the time for Ding had come to an end, yet One person had created the *Daiyan Calendar*, which was in error. Thus, the words of Luo Xiaohong were indeed true. One person also once visited the Taoist master Yin Chong to borrow *Yang Xiong's Great *Huangjing*. After several days, he returned the book to Yin Chong. Yin Chong said, "This book has profound and far-reaching meaning. I have been studying it for many years and still cannot understand it. My son, please try to study it further. Why did you return it so quickly?" One person said, "I have understood its meaning." He then took out the scroll he had written on the *Daiyan Huan Tu* and its commentary to show Yin Chong. Yin Chong greatly admired and bowed, saying to others, "This young man is like Yan Hui." When One person was young, he was poor, and there was a wealthy old woman named Wang who lived nearby. She found One person remarkable and often helped him with money and goods, assisting him in various ways, around several tens of thousands. One person often thought about repaying her. By the time of Emperor Xuanzong's reign, One person was highly respected and could say anything without hesitation. Not long after, Wang's son was arrested for murder, but the case was not yet resolved. Wang went to One person to ask for help. One person said, "If you want money and goods, I will pay ten times the amount. It is difficult to ask for leniency from the emperor who enforces the law." Wang slapped her hand and scolded loudly, "What use?"

Recognize this monk, Yixing, and follow to thank him, but finally he did not look back. Yixing, through his mental calculations, determined that there were hundreds of workers in the temple. He then ordered the room to be emptied and a large jar to be moved to the center. He secretly selected two of the temple's regular servants and gave them cloth bags, saying, "In a certain district and corner, there is an abandoned garden. You should wait there in hiding from noon to dusk. When seven animals enter, you can capture all of them. If you lose one, you will be beaten." As instructed, they went there, and by evening, a group of pigs indeed arrived, and all were captured and returned. Yixing was greatly pleased and ordered the pigs to be placed in the jar, covered with a wooden lid, sealed with six-one mud, and red Sanskrit characters were written on it. His disciples could not understand. The next morning, an imperial messenger knocked on the door and urgently summoned him to a side hall. Emperor Xuanzong greeted him and asked, "The Grand Historian reported that last night the Big Dipper was not visible. What is this omen? Do you have a way to ward it off?" Yixing replied, "During the Later Wei period, the planet Mars was lost. Since then, the imperial chariot has not been visible. This is something unheard of in ancient times. Heaven is issuing a great warning to Your Majesty. If common men and women are not treated fairly, there will be falling frost and red drought. Only the influence of great virtue can cause it to retreat. Those who are most deeply affected..."

During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, a person who had once been in a state of death or imprisonment entered the Buddhist realm, using their intense mind to disrupt all joy and their compassionate heart to subdue all demons. The emperor granted a general amnesty to the whole world, and Emperor Xuanzong agreed to it. On the same evening, the imperial astronomer reported that one star of the Big Dipper had appeared and would remain visible for seven days before returning. In the late Kaiyuan era, Pei Kuan, the governor of Henan, deeply believed in Buddhism and served as a disciple to the renowned Buddhist master Pu Ji. He visited him daily. One day, Pei Kuan went to see Pu Ji, who said, "I have some minor matters to attend to and have no time for a lengthy conversation. Please wait and rest for a while." Pei Kuan then remained silent in an empty room. He saw Pu Ji clean the main hall, burn incense, and sit upright. After a short while, he suddenly heard a knock on the door and heard a voice say, "Venerable Master Yi Xing has arrived." Yi Xing entered and paid his respects to Pu Ji. After the greetings, he whispered something into Pu Ji's ear. Yi Xing's demeanor was extremely respectful, and Pu Ji simply nodded, saying, "Nothing is impossible." After Yi Xing finished speaking, he descended the steps, entered the southern chamber, and closed the door. Pu Ji then calmly instructed his disciples to ring the bell, announcing that Venerable Master Yi Xing had passed away. The disciples rushed to check, and indeed, Yi Xing had passed away as predicted. After his passing, Pei Kuan wore mourning clothes and buried him, then walked out of the city to send him off.

唐玄宗已经任命牛仙客为宰相，颇为担忧时人的议论不一致，于是向高力士询问外界的看法如何。高力士说牛仙客出身于胥吏，不具备宰相的才能。皇帝大怒说，这就像康盖，这是皇帝一时气愤的话。有人私下报告说，皇帝对盖恩渥的恩宠很深，将来会任命他为宰相。盖听到后认为是真的。第二天，盖穿着盛装去上朝，已经就位，伸长脖子向北望，希望有任命。观看的人都忍不住笑，当时的人们也把他视为长者。盖担任将作大匠，多有巧思，尤其擅长地理，常对人说：“我住在这个宅子里，难道不能成为宰相吗？”听到的人更加嘲笑他。玄宗曾经命令教舞马四百蹄，各自分为左右，分为不同的部，名为某家宠、某家骄。当时塞外也有善马来进贡，皇帝命令他们教习，无不曲尽其妙。于是命令给他们穿上文绣，络以金银，装饰其鬃鬣，间杂。

The melody, like pearls and jade, is called *Qingbei Le*; the horses dance with flaring heads and thumping tails, moving in all directions in rhythm with the music. They ascend on horseback on a three-tiered platform, spinning like a whirlwind. Sometimes, a strong man is ordered to lift a bed on which the horses dance. Several musicians stand around, dressed in light yellow robes and jade belts, always seeking young and handsome performers. Every *Qianqiu* Festival, they are ordered to dance at the *Qingzheng* Pavilion. After the emperor fled to Shu, the dancing horses were scattered among the people. An Lushan often watched them dance and fell in love with them. From then on, he sold several of them to FanYang. Later, they were obtained by Tian Chengsi, who did not know their significance. Mixed with war horses and placed in an outer stable, one day during a feast in the army, the music started and the horses began to dance, unable to stop. The stable hands thought they were a demon and attacked them with brooms. However, the horses danced in a way that was still rhythmic and graceful, showing their old habits. The officials immediately reported this to Tian Chengsi, who ordered them to be severely beaten. The horses danced even more precisely, but the beating became more severe until they died. Some people knew they were dancing horses but were afraid of the violence and dared not speak. Emperor Xuanzong of Tang returned from Shu, late at night, he climbed the *Qingzheng* Pavilion and looked south, seeing mist and clouds everywhere. He sang a song, "The jade tree in the courtyard is already climbable, but the soldiers on the frontier have not returned for a long time." This is a poem by Lu Sida. After the song ended, the emperor asked if there were any old acquaintances. The next morning, he ordered them to be found. The next day, a strong man secretly searched the neighborhood and brought them back, and they turned out to be the *Liyuan* performers. That night, the emperor again climbed the pavilion under the moonlight, accompanied only by the strong man and the concubine Consort Yang's attendant, Red Peach. He then ordered them to sing *Liangzhou Ci*, composed by Consort Yang. The emperor personally played the jade flute to accompany the song. When the song ended, everyone present could not help but cry. The emperor then expanded the melody, and the version of *Liangzhou* that is passed down today is even more melancholic.

During the first year of the Zhude era, the former emperor Xuanzong visited the Huaqing Palace again. The local elders welcomed him with wine and food lining the roads. At that time, the emperor was advanced in age and often rode in a sedan chair. The elders approached and said, "When the former emperor passed through here, he often went hunting. Why don't you do so now?" The emperor replied, "I am old now; how could I possibly do that anymore?" The elders, officials, and women all heard this and could not help but weep. In Xinfeng Market, there was a female musician named Xie Aman, who was skilled at dancing the "Lingbo" dance. She often performed in the palace and was treated very kindly by Empress Yang Guifei. She also visited the residences of Yang Guozhong and his sisters. After Emperor Xuanzong fled to Sichuan, he traveled southwest, entering Xie Valley. He encountered continuous rain for ten days on the mountain road. Amid the rain, the sound of bells echoed through the mountains. The emperor, mourning the loss of Consort Yang, composed the "Rain on the Bell" melody from her voice to express his sorrow. At that time, among the imperial musicians, Zhang Yehu was the best at playing the suona. He accompanied the emperor to Sichuan, and the emperor taught him the "Rain on the Bell" melody. By the time of the Zhude era, when the emperor's entourage returned to the Huaqing Palace, most of the officials and concubines were not the same as before. The emperor ordered Zhang Yehu to perform the "Rain on the Bell" melody at the Pavilion of Gazing at the Capital. Before the melody was finished, the emperor looked around and felt desolate, shedding tears. Those around him were moved and sighed with him. The melody is now transmitted in the official music department.

During the Tianbao period, the princesses imitated each other in their extravagant feasts. The Empress appointed Yuan Siyi as the Inspector of Feasts, and the delicacies from land and sea amounted to thousands of dishes, costing the property of ten ordinary households. Du Hua, the Scribe of the Secretariat, once encountered the princesses' feast after court, as it was set up on the main thoroughfare. He passed through with shouts and reined in his horse, but hundreds of children of the imperial garden rushed forward with sticks, and Du Hua barely escaped with his life. On the birthday of Empress Wu Huifei, the emperor and the princesses performed a dance at the base of the Wansui Pavilion. The emperor rode in a palanquin and secretly observed from the overpass as the imperial guards finished eating and threw leftover buns and oranges into a water drain. The emperor was greatly angered and ordered Gao Lishi to beat them to death with a stick. Just as the emperor was furious, no one dared to speak. Prince Ning calmly asked the emperor, "If you secretly observed the minor fault of the imperial guards and had them killed, it might cause the officials to lose their composure and also lose the bigger picture. Your Majesty values frugality and cherishes things, yet you treat human life as less valuable than leftover food. How can you do this?" The emperor suddenly realized his mistake and immediately ordered a pardon. Afterward, Du Fu drifted and lived in the area around Xiangtan, traveling to Laiyang County in Hengzhou. He was quite annoyed by the county magistrate. Du Fu sent a poem to the magistrate, who then arranged a feast of roasted beef and white wine for him. Du Fu drank too much and died in one night.
